section.title: Double integrals over rectangles
section.p: built the double integral from a picture: columns over small rectangles. Now we make the pieces precise. The goal is the same as in single-variable calculus: define the integral in a way that does not depend on a lucky choice of sample points.

subsection.title: Partitions and sample points
subsection.p: Start with a rectangle R=[a,b]\times[c,d]. A partition of the x-interval is a list a=x_0\lt x_1\lt\cdots\lt x_m=b. A partition of the y-interval is a list c=y_0\lt y_1\lt\cdots\lt y_n=d. Together they cut R into subrectangles R_{ij}=[x_{i-1},x_i]\times[y_{j-1},y_j]. The area of R_{ij} is \Delta A_{ij}=\Delta x_i\,\Delta y_j, where \Delta x_i=x_i-x_{i-1}, \qquad \Delta y_j=y_j-y_{j-1}.

figure.caption: A partition cuts a rectangle into smaller rectangles. A Riemann sum chooses one sample point in each piece.

example.title: A midpoint estimate on a rectangle

statement.p:
Let R=[0,4]\times[0,2], and let f(x,y)=3+x+\frac12y. Cut R into four rectangles of size 2\times1. The midpoint sample points are (1,0.5),\quad (3,0.5),\quad (1,1.5),\quad (3,1.5). Each subrectangle has area \Delta A=2. The midpoint Riemann sum is
S \amp= 2\Bigl[ f(1,0.5)+f(3,0.5)+f(1,1.5)+f(3,1.5) \Bigr] \amp= 2\Bigl[ \left(3+1+\frac14\right) +\left(3+3+\frac14\right) +\left(3+1+\frac34\right) +\left(3+3+\frac34\right) \Bigr] \amp= 2\left[ \frac{17}{4}+\frac{25}{4}+\frac{19}{4}+\frac{27}{4} \right] \amp= 2\cdot 22 \amp= 44.
So this grid estimates \iint_R f\,dA by 44.

subsection.p: For this linear function, the midpoint estimate is already exact. We will prove such facts later using iterated integrals. For now, the sum is the object to understand.

subsection.title: Integrable functions, informally
subsection.p: A function is integrable over a rectangle when the Riemann sums settle down to one number as the rectangles get small.
The sample points should stop mattering. The exact shape of the grid should stop mattering. Only the function and the region should matter.

statement.p: A bounded function f:R\to\mathbb R on a rectangle R=[a,b]\times[c,d] is integrable over R if all Riemann sums \sum_i\sum_j f(x_{ij}^*,y_{ij}^*)\,\Delta A_{ij} approach the same number as the largest side length of the subrectangles approaches 0.

subsection.p: There is another way to say the same thing. On each subrectangle, the function has a largest possible value and a smallest possible value, at least for the functions we first meet. If we use all the largest values, we get an upper estimate. If we use all the smallest values, we get a lower estimate.
If the upper and lower estimates can be forced close together by using a fine enough partition, then the function is integrable.

theorem.title: Continuous functions on rectangles are integrable

statement.p: If f is continuous on a rectangle R=[a,b]\times[c,d], then f is integrable over R.

proof.title: Idea of proof
proof.p: A continuous function on a closed rectangle cannot oscillate too much inside very small rectangles. If the partition is fine enough, then the largest and smallest values of f on each subrectangle are close. Multiplying those small differences by the areas of the subrectangles and adding them gives upper and lower sums that are close. So every reasonable Riemann sum is trapped between two nearly equal numbers.

subsection.p: We required continuity because it gives a clean guarantee. Without it, the conclusion can fail.

example.title: A bounded function that is not integrable

statement.p: The function is bounded, but not integrable. It jumps too wildly on every tiny piece.

subsection.p: The boundedness condition is also part of this definition.

example.title: An unbounded function needs a different treatment

statement.p: This function does not fit the ordinary rectangle definition above. Integrals of unbounded functions require a limiting process. Those are improper double integrals, which return in .

subsection.p: The rectangle itself also has finite area. If we try to integrate f(x,y)=1 over the whole plane, the sums do not approach a finite number. They grow with the area being covered. Infinite regions also need improper integrals.
For now, rectangles are finite, functions are bounded, and continuity is the safe condition that makes the integral exist.

subsection.title: Average value over a rectangle
subsection.p: Single-variable calculus defined the average value of a function on an interval by f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx. The denominator was the length of the interval.
For a rectangle, length is replaced by area.
The units work out exactly as they should. If f is temperature in degrees Celsius, then \iint_R f\,dA has units {}^\circ\text{C}\cdot \text{m}^2. Dividing by area leaves {}^\circ\text{C}.

example.title: Average height of the market canopy

statement.p: In , the canopy over the rectangle [0,4]\times[0,3] had estimated volume 33\text{ m}^3. The floor area is 4\cdot 3=12\text{ m}^2. So the average canopy height is \frac{33}{12}=\frac{11}{4}=2.75\text{ m}. The average height is the height of a flat roof over the same floor that would enclose the same volume.

subsection.p: That last sentence is often the best way to remember average value:

subsection.title: Numerical estimates from tables and contour maps
subsection.p: In applications, we often do not know a formula for f(x,y). We may only have measurements.
Suppose a greenhouse floor is 0\le x\le 6,\qquad 0\le y\le 4, and temperature is measured at the centers of six equal rectangles. Each rectangle has dimensions 2\times2, so each has area 4.

table.title: Measured temperatures at the centers of six subrectangles.

subsection.p:
The midpoint estimate for the integral is
\iint_R T\,dA \amp\approx 4(18+19+20+22+24+23) \amp= 4(126) \amp= 504.
The total area is 6\cdot4=24. So the estimated average temperature is T_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{504}{24} = 21{}^\circ\text{C}.
This is a double integral computed from a table. The table supplies the sample values. The grid supplies the area pieces.
Contour maps give another kind of estimate. They do not give values at isolated points; they show curves of equal value. We can estimate by splitting the region into bands.

figure.caption: A contour map can be turned into an estimate by using the areas between contours.

example.title: Estimating water volume from depth bands

statement.p: A pond is divided by contour lines into depth bands. Suppose the estimated areas are:

table.title: Depth bands, typical depths, and estimated areas for the pond.

statement.p:
Then the water volume is estimated by
V \amp\approx 0.5(120)+1.5(200)+2.5(160)+3.5(60) \amp= 60+300+400+210 \amp= 970\text{ m}^3.
The pond area is 120+200+160+60=540\text{ m}^2. So the estimated average depth is \frac{970}{540}\approx 1.80\text{ m}.

subsection.p: Both the table method and the contour method are Riemann sums in disguise. Each small contribution has the form \text{value}\times\text{area}.

So far, our double integrals have been built by estimates. That is honest, but slow. Single-variable calculus became powerful when area sums turned into ordinary antiderivatives. The same thing happens here in a surprising way: a double integral over a rectangle can often be computed by doing two single-variable integrals, one after the other. The next step is to slice the rectangle into strips and let the old Fundamental Theorem of Calculus do part of the work.
